summary.zh: 鲜山水中的异蛇，蛇身生四翼，鸣声像击磬，它在哪座城邑现身，哪里就要大旱。与主水的化蛇同出伊水流域。
summary.en: A serpent of Mount Xian's waters with four wings and a call like a struck stone chime; wherever it appears, that town will suffer drought — counterpart to the flood-bringing Huashe.

description.zh:
《中次二经》记鲜山“多金玉，无草木”，鲜水从山中流出，北注伊水，水里多鸣蛇：“其状如蛇而四翼，其音如磬，见则其邑大旱。”蛇身上生着四只翅膀，叫起来像敲石磬，一声声清越；它在哪里露面，那座城邑随后就要大旱。

往西三百里的阳山水中住着化蛇，人面豺身鸟翼，见则其邑大水。同出伊水流域，一蛇主旱、一蛇主涝，是《中次二经》里方向相反的一对灾兆。四翼与磬音之外，经文再无一字，鸣蛇的全部形象就悬在这十几个字上；磬本是庙堂祭祀的石制乐器，这声正音偏偏出自旱兆之蛇的口中。
description.en:
Mount Xian carries gold and jade but no vegetation; the Xian River leaves it and runs north into the Yi. Its waters hold many Mingshe, 'singing snakes': serpents with four wings whose call rings like a struck stone chime. The consequence is fixed — wherever one is seen, that town will suffer a great drought.

Three hundred li to the west, the waters of Mount Yang hold the Huashe, a human-faced flood omen. Within the same Yi River basin the two serpents mark opposite disasters, drought and deluge, a matched pair of hydrological warnings in the second central sequence. Beyond the four wings and the chime-like voice the classic offers nothing more; the creature's whole image hangs on a dozen characters, and the chime — an instrument of temple ritual — sounds here from the mouth of a drought omen.

value.en: Mount Xian and the Xian River
